benchmark: Develop models to describe the atomic composition of simple molecules and extended structures.
item_specs: Students should be able to use models (ball-and-stick, space-filling) to represent molecules, identify atoms in compounds, and explain why models are limited representations of reality. Items may include diagrams requiring interpretation or construction.
essential_questions: How do atoms combine to form the substances around us?
Why do scientists use models to represent matter at the atomic scale?
What determines the properties of a substance at the molecular level?
learning_targets: I can identify the atoms present in simple molecules using chemical formulas and models.
I can develop and use models to describe how atoms are arranged in molecules and extended structures.
I can explain the limitations of models used to represent atomic and molecular structures.
sample_assessment: Draw a ball-and-stick model of water (H2O) and carbon dioxide (CO2). Label the atoms in each molecule and explain how the models show the composition of each substance.

benchmark: Analyze and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.
item_specs: Students analyze data tables or graphs showing properties (color, odor, temperature, mass, pH) before and after interactions to determine if a chemical reaction occurred. Items may present experimental data requiring interpretation.
essential_questions: How can we tell if a chemical reaction has occurred?
What evidence indicates that new substances have been formed?
How do the properties of reactants compare to the properties of products?
learning_targets: I can identify evidence that a chemical reaction has occurred (color change, gas production, temperature change, precipitate formation).
I can analyze and interpret data to distinguish between physical and chemical changes.
I can compare properties of substances before and after interactions to determine if new substances formed.
sample_assessment: A student mixes two clear solutions and observes a color change and a solid forming at the bottom of the beaker. Using the data table provided showing properties before and after mixing, determine whether a chemical reaction occurred and provide evidence to support your claim.

benchmark: Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society.
item_specs: Students use informational texts, data, or case studies to explain how synthetic materials are derived from natural resources and evaluate societal impacts. Items may include reading passages with comprehension questions.
essential_questions: Where do the materials used in everyday products come from?
How do synthetic materials affect society and the environment?
What trade-offs exist when choosing between natural and synthetic materials?
learning_targets: I can describe how synthetic materials are created from natural resources.
I can explain both positive and negative societal impacts of synthetic materials.
I can gather and evaluate information about how human-made materials affect daily life and the environment.
sample_assessment: Read the provided article about the production of plastic from petroleum. Identify the natural resource used, describe the process that converts it to a synthetic material, and explain two ways this synthetic material has impacted society — one positive and one negative.

benchmark: Develop a model that predicts and describes changes in particle motion, temperature, and state of a pure substance when thermal energy is added or removed.
item_specs: Students develop or analyze models (particle diagrams, graphs) showing how thermal energy changes particle motion, temperature, and state. Items may include temperature-time graphs with phase changes.
essential_questions: How does adding or removing thermal energy change the state of matter?
What happens to particles when a substance heats up or cools down?
Why do phase changes occur at specific temperatures for a given substance?
learning_targets: I can describe how particle motion changes when thermal energy is added to or removed from a substance.
I can predict the state of a pure substance at a given temperature using a heating or cooling curve.
I can develop a particle model that shows the differences between solids, liquids, and gases.
sample_assessment: Using the heating curve graph provided for water, identify the temperature at which ice melts and water boils. Draw particle diagrams showing the arrangement and motion of water molecules in the solid, liquid, and gas phases.

benchmark: Develop and use a model to describe how the total number of atoms does not change in a chemical reaction and thus mass is conserved.
item_specs: Students use models (particle diagrams, equation representations) to demonstrate conservation of atoms and mass in chemical reactions. Items include balancing simple equations conceptually or analyzing particle diagrams.
essential_questions: Why is mass conserved during a chemical reaction?
How do atoms rearrange during a chemical reaction?
What does it mean for a chemical equation to be balanced?
learning_targets: I can use particle models to show that atoms are rearranged but not created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.
I can explain why mass is conserved in a chemical reaction.
I can develop a model to represent how reactant atoms form product molecules in a balanced way.
sample_assessment: Use the particle diagrams provided to show how hydrogen and oxygen atoms combine to form water. Count the atoms on each side of the reaction and explain why the total mass of the products equals the total mass of the reactants.

benchmark: Undertake a performance task to investigate the factors that affect the rate of a chemical reaction.
item_specs: Students design or analyze investigations examining how concentration, temperature, surface area, or catalysts affect reaction rate. Items may require interpreting experimental data or identifying controlled variables.
essential_questions: What factors influence how fast a chemical reaction occurs?
How do scientists control variables when investigating reaction rates?
How can we use knowledge of reaction rates in practical applications?
learning_targets: I can identify variables (temperature, concentration, surface area, catalysts) that affect the rate of a chemical reaction.
I can design a controlled experiment to test one factor affecting reaction rate.
I can analyze data from an investigation to draw conclusions about what affects reaction rate.
sample_assessment: Design an experiment to determine how temperature affects the rate at which an effervescent tablet dissolves in water. Identify the independent variable, dependent variable, and at least two variables you would control. Describe how you would measure the reaction rate.

benchmark: Apply Newton's Third Law to design a solution to a problem involving the motion of two colliding objects.
item_specs: Students apply Newton's Third Law (action-reaction pairs) to analyze collisions and design engineering solutions. Items may include diagrams of collisions requiring identification of force pairs.
essential_questions: How do forces between objects affect each object's motion?
Why do action and reaction forces not cancel each other out?
How can understanding Newton's Third Law help engineers design better products?
learning_targets: I can identify action-reaction force pairs in collision scenarios.
I can apply Newton's Third Law to predict the effects of forces on two interacting objects.
I can design a solution to a collision problem using principles of Newton's Third Law.
sample_assessment: A car and a truck collide. Using Newton's Third Law, explain the forces each vehicle exerts on the other. Then describe a safety feature (such as crumple zones or airbags) that uses these principles to protect passengers, and explain how it works.

benchmark: Plan an investigation to provide evidence that the change in an object's motion depends on the sum of the forces on the object and the mass of the object.
item_specs: Students plan investigations or analyze data relating net force, mass, and acceleration. Items may include data tables or graphs requiring analysis of F=ma relationships.
essential_questions: How does the net force on an object affect its acceleration?
Why does mass matter when predicting how an object will move?
How do scientists test relationships between variables in motion investigations?
learning_targets: I can plan a controlled investigation to test how force and mass affect the motion of an object.
I can predict how changes in net force or mass will affect an object's acceleration.
I can analyze data to determine the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration.
sample_assessment: Plan an investigation using a toy car, a track, and different masses to determine how increasing force affects acceleration while keeping mass constant. Identify the variables, describe the procedure, and explain what data you would collect and how you would analyze it.

benchmark: Ask questions about data to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces.
item_specs: Students analyze data about electric charges and magnetic poles to determine factors (distance, charge magnitude, number of coils) affecting force strength. Items may include graphs showing inverse square relationships.
essential_questions: What factors determine the strength of electric and magnetic forces?
How are electric forces and magnetic forces similar and different?
How do electric and magnetic forces act at a distance?
learning_targets: I can identify factors that affect the strength of electric forces between charged objects.
I can identify factors that affect the strength of magnetic forces.
I can analyze data to determine relationships between distance, charge, and force strength.
sample_assessment: Study the data table showing the force between two charged objects at different distances. Describe the pattern you observe and write a question you could investigate to further explore how distance affects electric force strength.

benchmark: Construct and present arguments using evidence to support the claim that gravitational interactions are attractive and depend on the masses of interacting objects.
item_specs: Students construct evidence-based arguments about gravitational force. Items may include scenarios involving planets, moons, or everyday objects where students must apply understanding of gravitational attraction.
essential_questions: Why do objects with mass attract each other?
How does the mass of objects affect gravitational force between them?
How does gravity influence the motion of objects in the solar system?
learning_targets: I can explain that gravitational force is always attractive and acts between any two objects with mass.
I can describe how the mass of objects affects the strength of gravitational force between them.
I can construct an argument using evidence that supports the claim about gravitational interactions.
sample_assessment: Construct an argument using evidence from the data table provided (showing gravitational force between objects of different masses) to support the claim that gravitational force depends on the masses of the interacting objects. Include a counterclaim and rebuttal.

benchmark: Conduct an investigation and evaluate the experimental design to provide evidence that fields exist between objects exerting forces on each other even though the objects are not in contact.
item_specs: Students design or evaluate investigations using magnets or charged objects to demonstrate force-at-a-distance through fields. Items may include analysis of field diagrams or experimental procedures.
essential_questions: How can objects exert forces on each other without touching?
What is a field and how does it explain non-contact forces?
How do scientists provide evidence for things they cannot directly observe?
learning_targets: I can conduct an investigation to demonstrate that fields exist between objects that are not in contact.
I can evaluate an experimental design for its ability to provide evidence of fields.
I can represent field lines around magnets or charged objects and explain what they show.
sample_assessment: Design an investigation using two magnets and iron filings to provide evidence that a magnetic field exists around a magnet. Describe what you would observe, how you would record your data, and how your results would support the claim that fields exist between objects not in contact.

benchmark: Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to describe the relationships of kinetic energy to the mass and speed of an object.
item_specs: Students construct or interpret graphs showing kinetic energy vs. mass or speed. Items include data tables and graphs; students identify that KE increases proportionally with mass and with the square of speed.
essential_questions: What factors determine how much kinetic energy an object has?
How does doubling speed affect kinetic energy compared to doubling mass?
How do scientists use graphs to show relationships between variables?
learning_targets: I can construct a graph showing the relationship between kinetic energy and mass (at constant speed).
I can construct a graph showing the relationship between kinetic energy and speed (at constant mass).
I can interpret graphical data to compare how mass and speed affect kinetic energy.
sample_assessment: Using the data table showing kinetic energy values for objects of different masses moving at the same speed, construct a graph and describe the relationship. Then explain why a car moving at 60 mph has much more than twice the kinetic energy of the same car moving at 30 mph.

benchmark: Develop a model to describe that when the arrangement of objects interacting at a distance changes, different amounts of potential energy are stored in the system.
item_specs: Students develop models showing gravitational, elastic, or magnetic potential energy storage based on arrangement/distance. Items include diagrams or scenarios where students predict or explain energy storage changes.
essential_questions: How is energy stored in a system when objects interact at a distance?
What causes potential energy to change in a system?
How are kinetic and potential energy related in a system?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing how gravitational potential energy changes with height.
I can describe how elastic potential energy is stored when an object is deformed.
I can explain how the arrangement of interacting objects determines the amount of potential energy stored.
sample_assessment: Draw a model showing three positions of a ball: resting on the ground, held 1 meter above the ground, and held 3 meters above the ground. Label the potential energy stored in each position and explain what happens to this energy when the ball is released.

benchmark: Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer.
item_specs: Students apply understanding of conduction, convection, and radiation to design devices (insulators or conductors). Items include engineering design scenarios requiring trade-off analysis.
essential_questions: How can we control the transfer of thermal energy?
What materials are best for insulating versus conducting heat and why?
How do engineers use scientific principles to solve thermal energy problems?
learning_targets: I can apply knowledge of conduction, convection, and radiation to explain thermal energy transfer.
I can design and test a device that minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of my design and suggest improvements based on test results.
sample_assessment: Design a container to keep a warm liquid hot for as long as possible. Identify the materials you would use, explain how each material minimizes thermal energy transfer (conduction, convection, or radiation), and describe how you would test the effectiveness of your design.

benchmark: Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample.
item_specs: Students plan investigations examining how energy transfer affects temperature in different materials (specific heat concept). Items include designing controlled experiments with mass, material type, and energy as variables.
essential_questions: Why do different materials heat up at different rates when given the same amount of energy?
How is temperature related to the average kinetic energy of particles?
How do scientists investigate relationships between energy and temperature?
learning_targets: I can plan a controlled investigation to determine how energy transfer, mass, and material type affect temperature change.
I can explain the relationship between temperature and the average kinetic energy of particles.
I can predict how different materials will respond to the same amount of thermal energy input.
sample_assessment: Plan an investigation to compare how equal masses of water and sand respond to the same amount of thermal energy from a heat lamp. Identify the independent, dependent, and controlled variables, describe the procedure and measurements, and predict the results with a scientific explanation.

benchmark: Construct, use, and present arguments to support the claim that when the kinetic energy of an object changes, energy is transferred to or from the object.
item_specs: Students construct arguments connecting changes in kinetic energy to energy transfer through forces. Items may include scenarios of objects speeding up, slowing down, or being lifted, requiring energy transfer analysis.
essential_questions: Where does energy go when an object slows down?
How is work related to changes in kinetic energy?
What happens to total energy in a system when energy is transferred?
learning_targets: I can construct an argument that kinetic energy changes when energy is transferred to or from an object.
I can explain where energy comes from when an object speeds up and where it goes when an object slows down.
I can use evidence to support the claim that energy is conserved even when it changes forms.
sample_assessment: A soccer ball rolling across a field slows down and stops. Construct an argument supported by evidence to explain what happened to the kinetic energy of the ball. Identify where the energy was transferred and what evidence supports your claim.

benchmark: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in the wave.
item_specs: Students use diagrams and mathematical representations to describe wave properties. Items include wave diagrams where students measure or calculate amplitude, wavelength, and frequency and relate amplitude to energy.
essential_questions: What properties describe a wave and how are they measured?
How does the amplitude of a wave relate to the energy it carries?
How do scientists use mathematical models to describe waves?
learning_targets: I can identify and measure amplitude, wavelength, and frequency from a wave diagram.
I can explain the relationship between a wave's amplitude and the energy it carries.
I can use mathematical representations to describe wave properties and relationships.
sample_assessment: Using the wave diagram provided, measure the amplitude and wavelength of the wave. Then explain how you could modify the wave to carry more energy without changing the wavelength, and describe what that modified wave would look like.

benchmark: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials.
item_specs: Students develop or use models showing how waves interact with different materials (reflection, absorption, transmission). Items include diagrams of waves encountering boundaries or scenarios about light/sound behavior.
essential_questions: What happens when a wave encounters a new material?
Why can we see through some materials but not others?
How do reflection, absorption, and transmission apply to both light and sound waves?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing how waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted by different materials.
I can predict what will happen to a wave based on the properties of the material it encounters.
I can describe real-world examples of wave reflection, absorption, and transmission.
sample_assessment: Use diagrams to show what happens to light when it hits each of the following: a mirror, a black piece of paper, and a glass window. Label each process (reflection, absorption, or transmission) and explain the behavior of the light at each surface.

benchmark: Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information to support the claim that digitized signals are a more reliable way to encode and transmit information than analog signals.
item_specs: Students analyze texts and technical information comparing digital and analog signal reliability. Items may include reading passages, signal diagrams, or case studies about communication technology.
essential_questions: How is information encoded in digital and analog signals?
Why are digital signals more reliable for transmitting information?
How has digital technology changed the way we communicate?
learning_targets: I can describe the difference between digital and analog signals.
I can explain why digital signals are more reliable than analog signals for transmitting information.
I can integrate information from multiple sources to support a claim about digital versus analog communication.
sample_assessment: Read the provided texts about digital and analog audio recording. Integrate information from both sources to construct an argument supporting the claim that digital signals are more reliable for encoding and transmitting music. Include at least two pieces of evidence from the texts.

benchmark: Conduct an investigation to provide evidence that living things are made of cells; either one cell or many different numbers and types of cells.
item_specs: Students design or analyze investigations using microscopes to observe cells. Items may include microscope images or descriptions of observations requiring interpretation of cell types.
essential_questions: What is the basic unit of life and how do we know?
How are unicellular and multicellular organisms similar and different?
How did scientists gather evidence to develop cell theory?
learning_targets: I can conduct a microscopy investigation to observe cells in living things.
I can describe evidence that supports the claim that all living things are made of cells.
I can compare unicellular organisms to the cells of multicellular organisms.
sample_assessment: Describe the steps you would take to prepare and observe an onion skin sample under a microscope. What would you expect to see, and how would your observations provide evidence that plants are made of cells? Include a labeled drawing of what you would observe.

benchmark: Develop and use a model to describe the function of a cell as a whole and ways parts of cells contribute to that function.
item_specs: Students develop or use cell models identifying organelles and explaining their functions. Items include cell diagrams where students match organelles to functions or explain how structures enable cell activities.
essential_questions: How do the parts of a cell work together to keep it alive?
Why do different organelles have different shapes?
How is a cell like a city or factory?
learning_targets: I can identify major cell organelles and describe their functions.
I can develop a model to explain how the parts of a cell contribute to its overall function.
I can explain how the structure of each organelle relates to its function.
sample_assessment: Create an annotated diagram of an animal cell. For each labeled organelle (nucleus, mitochondria, cell membrane, ribosomes, endoplasmic reticulum), write a sentence explaining its function and how it contributes to the survival of the cell as a whole.

benchmark: Use argument supported by evidence for how the body is a system of interacting subsystems composed of groups of cells.
item_specs: Students construct arguments showing hierarchical organization (cell → tissue → organ → system → organism). Items may require describing interactions between body systems or explaining how disruption in one system affects others.
essential_questions: How are cells, tissues, organs, and body systems related?
How do the body's systems interact to maintain life?
What happens when one body system fails to function properly?
learning_targets: I can describe the levels of organization in the human body from cells to organ systems.
I can construct an argument using evidence that body systems interact with each other.
I can explain how specialized cells form tissues that make up organs and organ systems.
sample_assessment: Construct an argument using evidence that the digestive and circulatory systems interact to supply energy to body cells. Describe the role of each system and explain what evidence supports the claim that they work together as interacting subsystems.

benchmark: Use argument and evidence to support the claim that animals have evolved behaviors and physical structures that increase the likelihood of reproduction.
item_specs: Students analyze examples of animal behaviors and structures related to reproduction and construct evidence-based arguments about their adaptive value. Items may include case studies or data about reproductive success.
essential_questions: Why is reproduction critical for species survival?
How do animal behaviors increase the chances of successful reproduction?
What physical structures help animals attract mates or care for offspring?
learning_targets: I can describe animal behaviors that increase the likelihood of successful reproduction.
I can explain how physical structures in animals are related to reproductive success.
I can construct an argument using evidence that reproductive behaviors and structures are adaptations.
sample_assessment: Select one animal from the list provided (peacock, salmon, frog, or firefly). Construct an argument using evidence that at least one of its behaviors or physical structures increases its likelihood of successful reproduction. Cite specific evidence from the provided reading.

benchmark: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how environmental and genetic factors influence the growth of organisms.
item_specs: Students construct explanations using evidence about how genes and environment interact to affect growth. Items may include twin studies, plant growth experiments, or other data showing gene-environment interactions.
essential_questions: What determines how big an organism grows?
How do genes and the environment interact to shape an organism?
Why do genetically identical organisms sometimes look different?
learning_targets: I can construct a scientific explanation for how genetic factors influence organism growth.
I can construct a scientific explanation for how environmental factors influence organism growth.
I can describe examples showing that both genes and environment contribute to an organism's traits.
sample_assessment: A gardener plants seeds from the same plant (identical genetics) in two locations: one in full sun with rich soil and one in shade with poor soil. The plants grow to very different sizes. Construct a scientific explanation using evidence from the scenario to explain how environmental factors influenced the plants' growth.

benchmark: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for the role of photosynthesis in the cycling of matter and flow of energy into and out of organisms.
item_specs: Students construct explanations about photosynthesis as a process that transforms matter and transfers energy. Items include equations, data, or diagrams about photosynthesis requiring interpretation.
essential_questions: Where do plants get the matter and energy they need to grow?
How does photosynthesis connect the living and nonliving parts of an ecosystem?
How is the energy from the sun transformed and stored in living things?
learning_targets: I can construct a scientific explanation for how photosynthesis converts light energy to chemical energy.
I can describe how matter (carbon, hydrogen, oxygen) cycles through photosynthesis.
I can explain the role of photosynthesis in cycling matter and energy through ecosystems.
sample_assessment: Construct a scientific explanation using the photosynthesis equation (6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂) to describe how plants convert matter and energy. Explain where the atoms in the glucose come from and where the energy is stored.

benchmark: Develop a model to describe how food is rearranged through chemical reactions forming new molecules that support growth and/or release energy as this matter moves through an organism.
item_specs: Students develop models showing how food molecules are broken down and rearranged in cellular respiration and digestion. Items include diagrams or process models requiring students to trace matter and energy through an organism.
essential_questions: What happens to food after you eat it?
How do organisms release the energy stored in food?
How does matter from food become part of your body?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing how food is broken down and rearranged during digestion and cellular respiration.
I can explain how chemical reactions in cells release energy from food molecules.
I can trace how matter from food molecules is used for growth or energy release in an organism.
sample_assessment: Develop a flow diagram showing what happens to a glucose molecule after it enters a cell. Show the inputs and outputs of cellular respiration, identify where energy is released, and explain how this process supports the organism's growth and activity.

benchmark: Gather and synthesize information that sensory receptors respond to stimuli by sending signals to the brain for immediate behavior or storage as memories.
item_specs: Students gather and synthesize information about the nervous system's role in processing stimuli. Items may include reading passages or diagrams about sensory pathways requiring synthesis of information.
essential_questions: How does your body detect and respond to the environment?
What is the pathway from sensing a stimulus to responding to it?
How do sensory signals become memories or behaviors?
learning_targets: I can describe how sensory receptors detect stimuli in the environment.
I can explain how signals travel from sensory receptors to the brain.
I can describe the difference between immediate behavioral responses and memory formation.
sample_assessment: Using information from the provided text about the nervous system, synthesize an explanation of what happens in your body from the moment your hand touches a hot surface to the moment you pull it away. Include the roles of sensory receptors, neurons, and the brain in your explanation.

benchmark: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for the effects of resource availability on organisms and populations of organisms in an ecosystem.
item_specs: Students analyze graphs or data tables showing population changes in relation to resource availability. Items include data analysis tasks requiring students to identify patterns and draw conclusions about limiting factors.
essential_questions: How do resources limit the size of populations in an ecosystem?
What happens to a population when resources become scarce?
How do scientists study the effects of resource availability on ecosystems?
learning_targets: I can analyze data showing how resource availability affects organism growth and population size.
I can interpret graphs to identify patterns in population changes related to resources.
I can provide evidence-based explanations for how resources limit populations in ecosystems.
sample_assessment: Analyze the graph showing rabbit population size over 10 years in an ecosystem. During year 4, a drought reduced plant food by 60%. Describe the pattern you observe in rabbit population after year 4 and explain using evidence how resource availability affected the rabbit population.

benchmark: Construct an explanation that predicts patterns of interactions among organisms across multiple ecosystems.
item_specs: Students construct explanations predicting how predation, competition, mutualism, and other interactions affect ecosystems. Items may include food web diagrams requiring analysis of interaction effects.
essential_questions: How do the interactions between species shape ecosystems?
What patterns of interaction are found in different ecosystems around the world?
How does a change in one species affect others in a food web?
learning_targets: I can identify and describe patterns of interaction (predation, competition, mutualism, parasitism) in ecosystems.
I can construct an explanation predicting how changes in one population affect others in a food web.
I can compare patterns of species interactions across different ecosystems.
sample_assessment: Using the food web diagram provided for a forest ecosystem, predict what would happen to the deer population if wolves were reintroduced. Construct an explanation using the patterns of interaction in the food web to support your prediction.

benchmark: Develop a model to describe the cycling of matter and flow of energy among living and nonliving parts of an ecosystem.
item_specs: Students develop or use models (food chains, energy pyramids, nutrient cycle diagrams) showing matter cycling and energy flow. Items include ecosystem diagrams requiring interpretation or completion.
essential_questions: How does matter move through an ecosystem?
How does energy flow differently from matter in an ecosystem?
What roles do producers, consumers, and decomposers play in cycling matter?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing how matter cycles through the living and nonliving parts of an ecosystem.
I can describe how energy flows from producers to consumers in a food web.
I can explain the roles of producers, consumers, and decomposers in matter cycling and energy flow.
sample_assessment: Develop a model (diagram) of a pond ecosystem showing how carbon cycles through at least four organisms and the nonliving environment. Use arrows to show the flow of matter and label each process (photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, etc.).

benchmark: Construct an argument supported by empirical evidence that changes to physical or biological components of an ecosystem affect populations.
item_specs: Students construct arguments with empirical evidence about ecosystem disruptions (invasive species, habitat loss, pollution) and their effects on populations. Items may include case study data or historical population records.
essential_questions: How do changes to an ecosystem affect the organisms living in it?
What types of disturbances most significantly alter ecosystems?
How can scientists use evidence to show that ecosystem changes affect populations?
learning_targets: I can construct an argument using empirical evidence that physical changes in an ecosystem affect populations.
I can construct an argument using empirical evidence that biological changes (like invasive species) affect populations.
I can evaluate the strength of evidence used to support claims about ecosystem disruption.
sample_assessment: Using the data provided about lake trout populations before and after the introduction of sea lamprey to the Great Lakes, construct an argument that this biological change affected the lake trout population. Include at least two pieces of empirical evidence from the data.

benchmark: Evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services.
item_specs: Students evaluate multiple design solutions for conservation or ecosystem restoration, weighing criteria and constraints. Items may present two or more solutions requiring comparison using evidence.
essential_questions: Why is biodiversity important for ecosystem function?
How do scientists and engineers work together to protect ecosystems?
What trade-offs exist when designing solutions to protect biodiversity?
learning_targets: I can explain what biodiversity is and why it is important for ecosystems.
I can evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity using criteria and constraints.
I can describe ecosystem services and explain why they are valuable to humans.
sample_assessment: Two proposals are presented for protecting an endangered fish species: (1) a captive breeding program and (2) a habitat restoration project. Evaluate both solutions using criteria such as cost, effectiveness, and impact on biodiversity. Recommend one solution and support your recommendation with evidence.

benchmark: Develop and use a model to describe why structural changes to genes (mutations) located on chromosomes may affect proteins and may result in harmful, beneficial, or neutral effects to the structure and function of the organism.
item_specs: Students develop or use models showing how DNA mutations affect protein production and organism function. Items include gene-to-protein pathway scenarios where students predict mutation effects.
essential_questions: What is a mutation and how do mutations happen?
How does a change in DNA affect the proteins an organism makes?
Why do some mutations cause disease while others have no effect?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing the relationship between genes, proteins, and organism traits.
I can describe how a mutation (change in DNA) can affect the protein produced by a gene.
I can classify mutations as harmful, beneficial, or neutral and provide examples of each.
sample_assessment: A mutation changes one base in the gene that codes for hemoglobin, resulting in the protein folding differently. Develop a model showing how this mutation in the DNA leads to a change in the protein and explain why this particular mutation results in sickle cell disease (a harmful effect on the organism).

benchmark: Develop and use a model to describe why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information and sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation.
item_specs: Students compare asexual and sexual reproduction using models showing genetic outcomes. Items include diagrams requiring students to predict offspring genetics or explain sources of variation.
essential_questions: Why do offspring of sexual reproduction look different from their parents?
What are the advantages and disadvantages of sexual versus asexual reproduction?
How does genetic variation benefit a species?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing why asexual reproduction produces genetically identical offspring.
I can develop a model showing why sexual reproduction produces offspring with genetic variation.
I can explain the role of meiosis and fertilization in generating genetic variation.
sample_assessment: Draw two models side by side: one showing asexual reproduction (using binary fission) and one showing sexual reproduction in a simple organism. Label the genetic material in each parent and offspring. Explain why the offspring in each model are genetically identical or different from the parents.

benchmark: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.
item_specs: Students analyze fossil record data (timelines, diversity charts, extinction events) to identify patterns of evolution. Items include geologic time diagrams or data tables requiring pattern analysis.
essential_questions: What does the fossil record tell us about life on Earth?
How do scientists use fossils to understand evolutionary change?
What patterns of diversity and extinction appear in the fossil record?
learning_targets: I can analyze fossil record data to identify patterns of life form diversity, extinction, and change over time.
I can interpret a geologic timeline to describe how life on Earth has changed.
I can explain how the fossil record provides evidence for evolution.
sample_assessment: Examine the geologic timeline showing the appearance and extinction of major animal groups. Identify two patterns you observe in the fossil record and explain what these patterns suggest about the history of life on Earth. What major extinction events are visible in the data?

benchmark: Apply scientific ideas to construct an explanation for the anatomical similarities and differences among modern organisms and between modern and fossil organisms to infer evolutionary relationships.
item_specs: Students analyze anatomical comparisons (homologous structures, vestigial organs) and phylogenetic trees to infer evolutionary relationships. Items include diagrams of anatomy or phylogenetic trees requiring interpretation.
essential_questions: How can comparing anatomy help us understand evolutionary relationships?
Why do organisms that look very different sometimes share common ancestors?
What do homologous structures tell us about the history of life?
learning_targets: I can identify homologous structures in different organisms and use them to infer common ancestry.
I can interpret a phylogenetic tree to determine evolutionary relationships among organisms.
I can construct an explanation for similarities between modern organisms and fossil organisms.
sample_assessment: The diagram shows the forelimb bones of a human, bat, whale, and horse. Apply scientific ideas about evolution to construct an explanation for why these organisms have differently shaped limbs made of the same bones. What does this pattern of similarity suggest about their evolutionary history?

benchmark: Analyze displays of pictorial data to compare patterns of similarities in the embryological development across multiple species to identify relationships not evident in the fully formed anatomy.
item_specs: Students analyze embryo comparison diagrams showing developmental similarities across vertebrates. Items include embryo images or descriptions requiring pattern recognition and inference about evolutionary relationships.
essential_questions: Why do embryos of very different animals look so similar early in development?
What does embryological similarity tell us about evolutionary relationships?
How does embryological evidence complement other types of evolutionary evidence?
learning_targets: I can analyze embryo diagrams to identify similarities in developmental stages across species.
I can explain how similarities in embryological development provide evidence for common ancestry.
I can compare embryological evidence with anatomical evidence for evolutionary relationships.
sample_assessment: Examine the diagrams showing early, middle, and late embryonic stages of a fish, frog, chicken, and human. Identify at least two features that are similar across all species in the early stage but different in the late stage. Explain what these patterns suggest about the evolutionary relationships of these organisms.

benchmark: Construct an explanation based on evidence that describes how genetic variations of traits in a population increase some individuals' probability of surviving and reproducing in a specific environment.
item_specs: Students construct explanations connecting genetic variation, environmental conditions, and reproductive success. Items may include scenarios about populations with varying traits in changing environments.
essential_questions: Why do some individuals survive and reproduce while others do not?
How does natural selection lead to adaptations over time?
Why is genetic variation important for species survival?
learning_targets: I can construct an explanation for how genetic variation in a population leads to differential survival.
I can describe how environmental conditions determine which traits are advantageous.
I can explain how natural selection increases the frequency of beneficial traits in a population.
sample_assessment: A population of beetles has individuals with brown and green shells. Birds are predators that hunt by sight in a grassy environment. Construct an explanation using evidence for how natural selection would change the proportion of brown and green beetles over several generations in this environment.

benchmark: Gather and synthesize information about technologies that have changed the way humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.
item_specs: Students gather and synthesize information about selective breeding, genetic engineering, and other technologies. Items may include reading passages about modern biotechnology requiring synthesis and evaluation.
essential_questions: How have humans changed organisms through selective breeding?
How does modern biotechnology give humans more control over inheritance?
What ethical questions arise from human manipulation of genetic inheritance?
learning_targets: I can describe how selective breeding has changed the traits of domesticated plants and animals.
I can explain how genetic engineering technologies allow humans to influence inheritance.
I can synthesize information from multiple sources about technologies that affect inheritance.
sample_assessment: Gather information from the two provided texts about (1) traditional selective breeding of corn and (2) modern genetic engineering of corn. Synthesize this information to compare the two technologies, describing how each works and how each changes the inheritance of traits in corn.

benchmark: Use mathematical representations to support explanations of how natural selection may lead to increases and decreases of specific traits in populations over time.
item_specs: Students use data tables, graphs, or simple mathematical calculations to show how trait frequencies change over generations under natural selection. Items include population frequency data requiring quantitative analysis.
essential_questions: How can we measure evolutionary change in a population?
How quickly can natural selection change the traits of a population?
What mathematical patterns do we see when natural selection acts on a population?
learning_targets: I can use mathematical representations (graphs, percentages) to show how trait frequencies change over time.
I can explain how natural selection leads to increases in beneficial traits and decreases in harmful traits.
I can interpret population data to determine if natural selection is occurring.
sample_assessment: A population of 100 mice includes 60 white mice and 40 brown mice. In a snowy environment, predators catch white mice at a rate of 10% per generation and brown mice at 40% per generation. Use mathematical representations to calculate the frequency of each color after 3 generations and explain how natural selection is changing the population.

benchmark: Develop and use a model of the Earth-sun-moon system to describe the cyclic patterns of lunar phases, eclipses of the sun and moon, and seasons.
item_specs: Students develop or use models showing relative positions of Earth, sun, and moon to explain lunar phases, eclipses, and seasons. Items include diagrams requiring students to predict or explain cyclic phenomena.
essential_questions: Why does the moon appear to change shape throughout the month?
What causes solar and lunar eclipses?
Why do we have seasons on Earth?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing the positions of Earth, sun, and moon during different lunar phases.
I can explain how the relative positions of Earth, sun, and moon cause solar and lunar eclipses.
I can explain why Earth experiences seasons based on its tilted axis and orbit around the sun.
sample_assessment: Develop a diagram showing the positions of the Earth, moon, and sun during a new moon, full moon, first quarter, and third quarter. For each position, shade the moon to show how it appears from Earth. Then use a separate diagram to show the position of these three bodies during a solar eclipse.

benchmark: Develop and use a model to describe the role of gravity in the motions within galaxies and the solar system.
item_specs: Students develop or analyze models showing how gravity governs orbital motion in the solar system and galaxies. Items include scenario-based questions about gravitational effects on orbits.
essential_questions: What keeps planets in orbit around the sun?
How does gravity affect the motion of objects in the solar system and beyond?
Why don't planets fall into the sun or fly off into space?
learning_targets: I can develop a model to describe how gravity governs the motion of planets around the sun.
I can explain why objects orbit rather than fall directly toward or away from the massive body they orbit.
I can describe the role of gravity in organizing the structure of galaxies.
sample_assessment: Develop a model showing why the Earth stays in orbit around the sun rather than falling into it or flying off into space. Use the model to explain the role of gravity and inertia in maintaining Earth's orbit, and describe what would happen if the sun's gravitational force suddenly disappeared.

benchmark: Analyze and interpret data to determine scale properties of objects in the solar system.
item_specs: Students analyze data tables or scale models to compare sizes and distances in the solar system. Items include calculations requiring ratio or proportion, or interpretation of scale diagrams.
essential_questions: How does the size of Earth compare to other objects in the solar system?
Why is scale important when studying the solar system?
How do scientists measure and represent the vast distances in space?
learning_targets: I can analyze data to compare the sizes and distances of objects in the solar system.
I can use scale to create or interpret a model of the solar system.
I can describe the vast differences in size and distance between objects in the solar system.
sample_assessment: Using the data table showing the diameter and orbital distance of each planet, calculate how many times larger Jupiter is than Earth. Then create a scale model problem: if Earth were represented by a 1 cm sphere, what diameter would represent Jupiter and the sun? Explain what the scale model reveals about solar system proportions.

benchmark: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence from rock strata for how the geologic time scale is used to organize Earth's 4.6 billion-year-old history.
item_specs: Students construct explanations using rock strata, index fossils, and radiometric dating evidence to interpret Earth's history. Items include cross-sections of rock layers requiring interpretation and ordering of events.
essential_questions: How do scientists know how old Earth is?
What does the arrangement of rock layers tell us about Earth's history?
How is geologic time organized and why is that organization useful?
learning_targets: I can construct a scientific explanation for how rock strata provide evidence of Earth's history.
I can describe how the geologic time scale is organized and what events mark its major divisions.
I can explain how scientists use evidence from rock layers and fossils to determine the age of Earth's features.
sample_assessment: Examine the diagram showing five horizontal rock layers with fossils. Layer A is at the bottom and layer E is at the top. Using the law of superposition and the index fossils identified in each layer, construct an explanation for the sequence of events that formed these rock layers and estimate the relative age of each layer.

benchmark: Develop a model to describe the cycling of Earth's materials and the flow of energy that drives this process.
item_specs: Students develop or use models of the rock cycle showing transformation of rock types driven by energy. Items include rock cycle diagrams requiring students to identify processes and energy sources.
essential_questions: How do rocks change from one type to another?
What sources of energy drive Earth's rock cycle?
How are the three types of rock related to each other?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing how Earth's materials cycle through igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rock types.
I can identify the energy sources (heat from Earth's interior, solar energy) that drive the rock cycle.
I can describe the processes (melting, crystallization, erosion, pressure, heat) that transform rocks.
sample_assessment: Develop a rock cycle model (diagram) showing at least four steps in the cycling of Earth's materials. For each step, name the process, identify the type of rock being formed, and label the energy source (internal heat or solar energy) that drives that process.

benchmark: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how geoscience processes have changed Earth's surface at varying time and spatial scales.
item_specs: Students construct explanations comparing fast and slow geologic processes (erosion, weathering, volcanic eruption, glacial movement). Items include scenario or evidence-based analysis of surface changes.
essential_questions: How has Earth's surface changed over time?
Which geologic processes are fast and which are slow?
How do different geologic processes shape Earth's surface features?
learning_targets: I can construct an explanation for how slow processes (erosion, deposition) change Earth's surface over long time scales.
I can construct an explanation for how rapid processes (earthquakes, volcanic eruptions) change Earth's surface quickly.
I can compare the rates and scales of different geoscience processes using evidence.
sample_assessment: The Colorado River has carved the Grand Canyon over millions of years, while the 1980 eruption of Mt. St. Helens transformed the surrounding landscape in hours. Construct an explanation based on evidence for how both slow and rapid geoscience processes have changed Earth's surface, including the time and spatial scale of each process.

benchmark: Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions.
item_specs: Students analyze maps of fossil distributions, rock types, and seafloor features to infer past plate movement. Items include world maps or data requiring interpretation of tectonic evidence.
essential_questions: What evidence supports the theory of plate tectonics?
How do we know that continents have moved over time?
What does the distribution of fossils and rocks tell us about past plate positions?
learning_targets: I can analyze fossil and rock distribution maps to identify evidence of past plate motions.
I can interpret seafloor spreading data as evidence of plate movement.
I can describe multiple lines of evidence that support the theory of plate tectonics.
sample_assessment: Examine the world map showing the distribution of the fossil plant Glossopteris and the reptile Mesosaurus on today's continents. Analyze this data along with the continental shapes and identify what these patterns suggest about past plate positions. Provide evidence for the claim that these continents were once connected.

benchmark: Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth's systems driven by energy from the sun and the force of gravity.
item_specs: Students develop or use water cycle models showing evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, and groundwater driven by solar energy and gravity. Items include water cycle diagrams requiring labeling or explanation.
essential_questions: What drives the movement of water through Earth's systems?
How does water move between the atmosphere, land, and oceans?
How does the water cycle connect different parts of Earth's systems?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing how water cycles through Earth's atmosphere, land, and oceans.
I can identify the role of solar energy in driving evaporation and the water cycle.
I can describe how gravity moves water downhill as runoff and through soil as groundwater.
sample_assessment: Develop a water cycle diagram that shows the pathway of a water molecule from the ocean to a mountain glacier and back to the ocean. Label each process (evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, etc.) and for each step, identify whether solar energy or gravity is the driving force.

benchmark: Collect data to provide evidence for how the motions and complex interactions of air masses result in changes in weather conditions.
item_specs: Students collect or analyze weather data (temperature, pressure, humidity, wind) and connect patterns to air mass interactions and fronts. Items may include weather maps or data tables.
essential_questions: Why does weather change from day to day?
How do the interactions of air masses create weather?
How do scientists collect and use weather data to make forecasts?
learning_targets: I can collect and analyze weather data to identify patterns in temperature, pressure, and precipitation.
I can describe how the interaction of warm and cold air masses creates weather changes.
I can explain how fronts form and what weather typically follows each type of front.
sample_assessment: Examine the weather map showing a cold front moving through a region. Using the temperature, pressure, and precipitation data provided for 5 cities in the front's path, describe the weather changes that occurred before, during, and after the front passed through each city. What pattern do you observe?

benchmark: Develop and use a model to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth cause patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation that determine regional climates.
item_specs: Students develop or use models showing global circulation patterns (Hadley cells, ocean currents) and their effects on regional climate. Items include climate and circulation maps requiring pattern analysis.
essential_questions: Why do different parts of Earth have different climates?
How does the unequal heating of Earth drive atmospheric and ocean circulation?
What role does Earth's rotation play in global wind patterns?
learning_targets: I can develop a model showing how unequal heating of Earth's surface drives atmospheric circulation.
I can explain how Earth's rotation affects the direction of global wind patterns.
I can describe how atmospheric and oceanic circulation patterns determine regional climates.
sample_assessment: Develop a model (diagram) of Earth showing how unequal solar heating between the equator and poles creates global atmospheric circulation cells. Label the direction of surface winds in each cell and explain how Earth's rotation causes winds to curve. Then explain why coastal cities often have milder climates than inland cities at the same latitude.

benchmark: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how the uneven distributions of Earth's mineral, energy, and groundwater resources are the result of past and current geoscience processes.
item_specs: Students construct explanations connecting natural resource distribution to geological processes. Items may include resource distribution maps or process descriptions requiring explanation of formation.
essential_questions: Why are natural resources unevenly distributed around the world?
How do geological processes create concentrations of minerals and fossil fuels?
How does the uneven distribution of resources affect human societies?
learning_targets: I can construct a scientific explanation for why mineral resources are unevenly distributed on Earth.
I can explain how geoscience processes (plate tectonics, sedimentation) create concentrations of energy resources.
I can describe how groundwater resources form and why their distribution is uneven.
sample_assessment: Coal deposits are found in Pennsylvania while copper deposits are concentrated in the American Southwest. Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how past geoscience processes led to the formation and distribution of these two different natural resources in different locations.

benchmark: Analyze and interpret data on natural hazards to forecast future catastrophic events and inform the development of technologies to mitigate their effects.
item_specs: Students analyze historical data on earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, hurricanes, or floods to identify patterns and inform hazard mitigation. Items include maps or historical records requiring pattern analysis for prediction.
essential_questions: How do scientists predict natural disasters?
What technologies help communities prepare for natural hazards?
How can understanding patterns of natural hazards save lives?
learning_targets: I can analyze historical data to identify patterns in natural hazard occurrence.
I can use data patterns to make predictions about future natural hazard events.
I can describe technologies designed to mitigate the effects of natural hazards.
sample_assessment: Analyze the earthquake frequency data for the Pacific Rim over the past 50 years shown in the table. Identify the geographic pattern of earthquake activity, and use this data to predict which regions are at highest risk for future earthquakes. Describe one technology that communities in these high-risk areas use to reduce earthquake damage.

benchmark: Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing a human impact on the environment.
item_specs: Students apply scientific principles to design monitoring systems or minimization strategies for human environmental impacts. Items include engineering design scenarios with criteria and constraints.
essential_questions: How do human activities impact Earth's environment?
How do scientists monitor environmental changes caused by humans?
What methods can minimize human impacts on the environment?
learning_targets: I can identify specific ways human activities negatively impact the environment.
I can apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring an environmental impact.
I can design and evaluate strategies for minimizing human impact on the environment.
sample_assessment: A factory is releasing chemicals into a nearby river. Apply scientific principles to design a monitoring system for detecting chemical pollution levels in the river. Describe the type of data you would collect, the equipment you would use, how often you would sample, and at how many locations. Then propose one method to minimize the factory's impact.

benchmark: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems.
item_specs: Students construct evidence-based arguments about population growth and resource consumption trends. Items may include population graphs or resource use data requiring quantitative analysis and argumentation.
essential_questions: How does population growth affect the demand for natural resources?
What is per-capita consumption and why does it matter?
How do human population and consumption patterns threaten Earth's systems?
learning_targets: I can construct an argument using data about how population growth increases resource consumption.
I can explain the concept of per-capita consumption and how it affects resource depletion.
I can describe specific examples of how increased human population and consumption impact Earth's systems.
sample_assessment: Using the provided graphs showing world population growth and freshwater consumption from 1950 to 2020, construct an argument supported by evidence that increases in human population and per-capita consumption are impacting Earth's water systems. Include specific data points from both graphs in your argument.

benchmark: Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over the past century.
item_specs: Students develop and use questions to investigate evidence for anthropogenic climate change (CO₂ levels, temperature data, ice cores). Items include data sets requiring interpretation and questioning strategies.
essential_questions: What evidence shows that global temperatures have risen over the past century?
What factors are causing Earth's temperatures to rise?
How do scientists investigate the causes of climate change?
learning_targets: I can identify and describe evidence (temperature records, CO₂ data, sea level rise) for global temperature rise.
I can ask scientific questions that clarify the factors causing global temperature rise.
I can distinguish between natural factors and human activities as causes of climate change.
sample_assessment: Examine the graph showing average global temperature anomalies and atmospheric CO₂ levels from 1880 to 2020. Develop three scientific questions you would investigate to clarify the relationship between CO₂ levels and global temperature rise. Then identify two pieces of evidence from the graph that suggest human activities are contributing to global temperature increases.

benchmark: Define the criteria and constraints of a design problem with sufficient precision to ensure a successful solution, taking into account relevant scientific principles and potential impacts on people and the natural environment that may limit possible solutions.
item_specs: Students define engineering design problems by identifying criteria, constraints, and relevant scientific principles. Items may present real-world scenarios requiring students to develop well-defined problem statements.
essential_questions: What is the difference between a criterion and a constraint in engineering?
How do scientific principles limit or guide engineering solutions?
Why is defining a problem precisely important before designing a solution?
learning_targets: I can define the criteria (what a successful solution must do) for an engineering design problem.
I can identify constraints (limitations such as cost, materials, time) that limit possible solutions.
I can explain how scientific principles and potential environmental impacts inform the problem definition.
sample_assessment: A community needs a bridge over a 10-meter wide river that can support vehicles, costs no more than $50,000, and must not interfere with fish migration. Define the design problem by listing at least three criteria and three constraints, and explain one scientific principle that engineers must consider in designing this bridge.

benchmark: Evaluate competing design solutions using a systematic process to determine how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
item_specs: Students use evaluation matrices or systematic processes to compare design solutions against criteria and constraints. Items present multiple solutions requiring evaluation using specified criteria.
essential_questions: How do engineers decide which design solution is best?
What is a fair and systematic way to compare different design solutions?
How do trade-offs affect engineering decisions?
learning_targets: I can evaluate multiple design solutions using a decision matrix or systematic comparison process.
I can determine how well each design solution meets the established criteria and constraints.
I can identify trade-offs in competing design solutions and make evidence-based recommendations.
sample_assessment: Three designs for a solar-powered water heater are presented, each with different costs, efficiencies, and durability ratings. Create a decision matrix using the criteria (cost under $200, efficiency above 70%, lasts 10+ years) and constraints provided. Evaluate each design and recommend the best solution with justification.

benchmark: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.
item_specs: Students analyze test data from multiple design prototypes to identify strengths of each and combine them into an improved design. Items include performance data requiring analysis and synthesis.
essential_questions: How do engineers use test results to improve their designs?
What can we learn from designs that partially meet the criteria?
How does combining features from different designs lead to a better solution?
learning_targets: I can analyze test data to identify the strengths and weaknesses of different design solutions.
I can identify the best characteristics of each design that should be incorporated into a new solution.
I can describe how an improved design combines the best features of multiple tested solutions.
sample_assessment: Three prototype water filters were tested for flow rate, contaminant removal, and cost. Design A has excellent flow rate but poor contaminant removal. Design B removes contaminants well but is slow. Design C is cheap but inconsistent. Analyze the data and describe a new design that combines the best characteristics of each prototype to better meet all three criteria.

benchmark: Develop a model to generate data for iterative testing and modification of a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.
item_specs: Students develop models or simulations to test and iteratively improve designs. Items may include engineering scenarios where students must identify what to modify based on model outputs.
essential_questions: How do models help engineers test designs before building them?
Why is iteration important in engineering design?
How do engineers know when a design is optimized?
learning_targets: I can develop a model that generates testable data for an engineering design.
I can use data from model testing to identify specific modifications that improve the design.
I can describe how iterative testing and modification leads to an optimal design solution.
sample_assessment: You are designing a parachute to safely land an egg dropped from 3 meters. Develop a model for testing your parachute design by describing (1) what you would build and measure, (2) what data you would collect from each test, (3) what modifications you would make based on the data, and (4) how you would know when you have achieved an optimal design.

benchmark: Use the periodic table as a model to predict the relative properties of elements based on the patterns of electrons in the outermost energy level of atoms.
item_specs: Students should be able to interpret periodic trends (atomic radius, ionization energy, electronegativity) using electron shell models. Items may ask students to compare element properties or predict reactivity based on group/period position.
essential_questions: How does the arrangement of electrons in an atom determine its chemical properties?
Why do elements in the same group of the periodic table behave similarly?
How can we use the periodic table to predict properties of unfamiliar elements?
learning_targets: I can explain how the number of valence electrons determines an element's chemical behavior.
I can use periodic trends to predict relative atomic radius, ionization energy, and electronegativity.
I can identify patterns in the periodic table and use them to compare element properties.
sample_assessment: Using the periodic table, predict which element — sodium (Na) or chlorine (Cl) — has a higher ionization energy and explain why based on electron configuration.

benchmark: Construct and revise an explanation for the outcome of a simple chemical reaction based on the outermost electron states of atoms, trends in the periodic table, and knowledge of the patterns of chemical properties.
item_specs: Students should predict products of simple reactions and explain outcomes using electron configuration and periodic trends. Items may include ionic and covalent bond formation scenarios.
essential_questions: Why do certain elements readily react with each other while others do not?
How do electron configurations determine the type of bond formed between atoms?
How can periodic trends help us predict the products of a chemical reaction?
learning_targets: I can predict whether atoms will form ionic or covalent bonds based on electronegativity differences.
I can explain how valence electrons drive chemical reactions.
I can construct an explanation for the outcome of a simple chemical reaction using electron state information.
sample_assessment: Explain why sodium and chlorine react vigorously using their electron configurations. What type of bond forms and why?

benchmark: Plan and conduct an investigation to gather evidence to compare the structure of substances at the bulk scale to infer the strength of electrical forces between particles.
item_specs: Students design or evaluate investigations comparing melting points, boiling points, or solubility to infer intermolecular force strength. Items may include data tables and require interpretation.
essential_questions: How does the structure of a substance at the molecular level affect its physical properties?
What evidence can we gather at the bulk scale to infer forces between particles?
Why do substances with similar molecular masses sometimes have very different boiling points?
learning_targets: I can plan an investigation to compare physical properties of substances and relate them to intermolecular forces.
I can analyze bulk-scale data (melting point, boiling point) to infer particle-level interactions.
I can explain why hydrogen bonding leads to higher boiling points compared to dispersion forces.
sample_assessment: A student tests the boiling points of water (100°C), ethanol (78°C), and propane (-42°C). Explain the differences in boiling points using intermolecular force theory.

benchmark: Develop a model to illustrate that the release or absorption of energy from a chemical reaction system depends upon the changes in total bond energy.
item_specs: Students use bond energy tables or diagrams to calculate net energy change in reactions. Items may ask students to classify reactions as exothermic or endothermic and justify using bond breaking/forming.
essential_questions: Why do some chemical reactions release energy while others absorb it?
How is the energy stored in chemical bonds related to the heat released or absorbed in a reaction?
How can we model energy changes in a chemical reaction?
learning_targets: I can use a bond energy table to calculate the net energy change in a chemical reaction.
I can develop a model showing energy stored in reactant bonds versus product bonds.
I can classify a reaction as exothermic or endothermic based on total bond energy changes.
sample_assessment: Using bond energies, calculate the enthalpy change for the combustion of methane (CH4 + 2O2 → CO2 + 2H2O). Is the reaction exothermic or endothermic?

benchmark: Apply scientific principles and evidence to provide an explanation about the effects of changing the temperature or concentration of the reacting particles on the rate at which a reaction occurs.
item_specs: Students apply collision theory to explain how temperature, concentration, surface area, and catalysts affect reaction rate. Items may include graphs of concentration vs. time.
essential_questions: What factors determine how quickly a chemical reaction proceeds?
How does collision theory explain the effect of temperature on reaction rate?
Why does increasing concentration speed up a reaction?
learning_targets: I can apply collision theory to explain how temperature and concentration affect reaction rate.
I can analyze a reaction rate graph and identify how variables affect the speed of a reaction.
I can predict how changing conditions (temperature, concentration, surface area) will affect reaction rate.
sample_assessment: A student measures the rate of reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid at 20°C and 40°C. Explain why the reaction at 40°C is faster using collision theory.

benchmark: Refine the design of a chemical system by specifying a change in conditions that would shift the equilibrium toward products through application of Le Chatelier's Principle.
item_specs: Students apply Le Chatelier's Principle to predict equilibrium shifts when concentration, temperature, or pressure is changed. Items may include Haber process or other industrial applications.
essential_questions: How does a chemical system at equilibrium respond to changes in conditions?
How can chemists manipulate equilibrium to maximize product yield?
What does it mean for a chemical reaction to be in dynamic equilibrium?
learning_targets: I can predict how a chemical equilibrium will shift when concentration, temperature, or pressure is changed.
I can apply Le Chatelier's Principle to explain industrial optimization of chemical reactions.
I can design conditions that shift equilibrium toward desired products.
sample_assessment: In the Haber process (N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3, exothermic), predict what happens to ammonia yield if temperature is increased. Justify using Le Chatelier's Principle.

benchmark: Use mathematical representations to support the claim that atoms, and therefore mass, are conserved during a chemical reaction.
item_specs: Students balance chemical equations and use mole ratios for stoichiometric calculations. Items may include limiting reagent problems or percent yield calculations.
essential_questions: Why must atoms be conserved during a chemical reaction?
How can mathematics be used to track atoms through a chemical reaction?
How do chemists use balanced equations to predict amounts of reactants and products?
learning_targets: I can balance chemical equations to demonstrate conservation of mass.
I can use mole ratios from a balanced equation to calculate amounts of reactants and products.
I can solve stoichiometry problems including limiting reagent and percent yield.
sample_assessment: Balance the equation for the combustion of propane (C3H8 + O2 → CO2 + H2O) and calculate how many grams of CO2 are produced from 44g of propane.

benchmark: Develop models to illustrate the changes in the composition of the nucleus of the atom and the energy released during the processes of fission, fusion, and radioactive decay.
item_specs: Students develop or interpret models showing nuclear changes in fission, fusion, and decay. Items may ask students to write decay equations or compare energy released in nuclear vs. chemical reactions.
essential_questions: How does the nucleus of an atom change during fission, fusion, and radioactive decay?
Why do nuclear reactions release so much more energy than chemical reactions?
What are the societal implications of harnessing nuclear energy?
learning_targets: I can model the changes in proton and neutron number during alpha decay, beta decay, and gamma emission.
I can compare fission and fusion reactions and explain the energy released in each.
I can write nuclear equations and verify conservation of mass number and atomic number.
sample_assessment: Write the nuclear equation for the alpha decay of uranium-238. What new element is formed? How does the energy released compare to a chemical combustion reaction?

benchmark: Analyze data to support the claim that Newton's second law of motion describes the mathematical relationship among the net force on a macroscopic object, its mass, and its acceleration.
